title: Quantum-enabled complete RF-polarimetry with an optically-wired atomic sensor
authors: Matthew Chilcott, Laurits N. Stokholm, Matthew Cloutman, J. Susanne Otto, Amita B. Deb, Niels Kjærgaard
abstract: Rydberg atomic electrometry leverages the extreme sensitivity of highly excited atoms for calibration-free electric field measurements. The technique uses a non-metallic vapor cell to link properties of an RF field to a spectroscopic readout in the optical domain. Most demonstrations have so far focused on detecting linearly-polarized fields, for which the induced splitting of dressed atomic levels is rotationally invariant. Here we report on Rydberg atomic measurements of RF fields in a general state of polarization (SOP) which we map onto the Poincaré sphere through spectroscopic fingerprints. For a Stokes vector circumnavigating a Poincaré sphere meridian, we witness a continuous transformation of the atomic eigenenergy spectrum. Because the relative positions of eigenenergies are locked in place by quantization of angular momentum, the framework is universal and calibration free. We provide a specific demonstration in rubidium, which generalizes to all systems with a single valence electron.
summary: This paper presents a method for complete RF-polarimetry using Rydberg atoms as an optically-wired sensor. By analyzing the spectroscopic fingerprints of RF-dressed atoms, the researchers can determine the full polarization state of an RF field without needing calibration or metallic antennas. This approach leverages the fundamental quantization of angular momentum to create a universal sensing framework.
main_problem: Developing a complete, calibration-free RF-polarimetry method to determine the full Stokes vector of an RF field using Rydberg atoms without the field distortions caused by metallic antennas.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that the state of polarization (SOP) of an RF field is encoded in the spectroscopic fingerprints of Rydberg atoms, allowing for universal, calibration-free measurement of the full Stokes vector.
method: The method uses Electromagnetically Induced Transparency (EIT) in a Rubidium vapor cell to probe RF-dressed Rydberg states, employing an analytical inversion of the EIT spectra based on eigenvalue envelope ratios.
model_or_system: An optically-wired atomic sensor using 87Rb atoms in a vapor cell, specifically utilizing Rydberg states (n approx 50) and a ladder-type EIT scheme with 780 nm and 480 nm lasers.
key_observables: Atomic eigenenergy spectrum, EIT peak positions, and the eigenvalue envelope ratio R.
important_parameters: RF phase angle (phi), RF polarization (linear, circular, elliptical), and principal quantum number (n).
main_assumptions: The use of the dipole approximation and the rotating wave approximation (RWA).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mapping of phase angles to EIT spectra, the identification of mathematical ambiguities in the phase angle extraction, and the use of peak intensity/symmetry breaking to resolve those ambiguities.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of RF-polarimetry, describes the Rydberg-based experimental platform, presents the analytical framework for mapping SOP to the Poincaré sphere, details the inversion method and ambiguity resolution, and concludes with experimental demonstrations in Rubidium.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and AMO physics as it demonstrates a novel way to use the internal quantum structure (angular momentum quantization) of atoms to perform high-precision, non-invasive sensing of external classical fields.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 | Rydberg atomic SOP analyzer. a, Schematic for SOP measurement. The RF field produced by a dual-polarized ( ˆ u1/ ˆ u2) lensed horn antenna propagates in the z-direction towards an atomic vapor cell located at the origin. Coupling and probe laser beams counter-propagate along the y-axis. b, Poincar´e sphere showing the trajectory of the RF-field’s Stokes vector (red arrow) when ϕ = 0 →2π in equation (1). As ϕ varies, the polarization state evolves from linear-vertical (LVP) to left-circular (LCP) to linear-horizontal (LHP) to right-circular (RCP), and back to LVP.

Fig. 5 | EIT spectra for atoms dressed with circularly polarized RF fields. a, Experimental spectrogram for a field-dressed ↕3/2 5/2-transition probed in the configuration Fig. 1a with dashed light and dark green lines marking the sections of LCP (ϕ = π/2) and RCP (ϕ = 3π/2) RF-fields. b,c, The nominally identical spectra recorded for LCP and RCP RF- fields.

Fig. 6 | Breaking spectrogram symmetry. Effect of altering the optical configuration when EIT-probing a field-dressed ↕5/2 3/2-transition. a, Simulated spectrogram for optical fields linearly polarized along ˆx and counter-propagating in the yz-plane; the spectrogram is invariant under a rotation of the optical beam direction about ˆx. The spectra for shown in the lower panels are indistinguishable. b, Simulated spectrogram for circularly polarized optical probe and coupling beams propagating along ˆy −ˆz. From the spectra shown in the low panel it is clearly possible to discriminate between LCP and RCP RF dressing fields. c, Experimental realization of the optical configuration of b.
